task: I’m looking at a used truck deal and the seller keeps pointing to the title, maintenance records, and a verbal promise about handling any major issue after the sale. What are the main details you’d want to verify before moving forward so the paperwork and the conversation stay aligned?

prefs.explanation: He seems most comfortable with a businesslike interaction style rather than friendliness for its own sake. Given his contract-focused, guarded approach, overly casual wording would likely feel sloppy or untrustworthy. He would probably respond best to a composed tone that stays respectful and efficient.

prefs.short: Wants concise answers with no padding
prefs.explanation: He likely prefers responses that get to the point quickly and avoid unnecessary commentary. His practical, control-oriented style suggests low patience for rambling explanations or emotional framing. If a topic needs detail, he would still want it trimmed to only what changes the decision.

prefs.short: Likes bullet points and structured output
prefs.explanation: He appears to favor information that is organized, easy to scan, and hard to misread. Lists, checklists, or clearly separated sections would fit his record-keeping habits better than loose prose. Unstructured answers probably feel inefficient to him, especially when he is trying to act on something.

He seems unlikely to want hedging or excessive politeness when the point is clarity. His guarded pragmatism fits a style that states the issue plainly, especially when there is risk or cost involved. Softened language may read to him as evasive or manipulative rather than considerate.

prefs.short: Wants firm pushback on bad calls
prefs.explanation: He is probably receptive to being challenged when the challenge is concrete and tied to risk. Because he already thinks in terms of exposure and downside, he may value an assistant that flags weak assumptions instead of simply agreeing. He would likely dislike vague disagreement, but accept hard pushback if it is specific and justified.

turns.agent_response:
Yes — I’d verify these first:

## 1) Title and VIN
- title is in the seller’s name
- VIN matches the truck everywhere
- mileage matches the title and odometer
- no salvage / rebuilt / lien issue unless fully disclosed

## 2) Maintenance records
- oil changes
- transmission service
- major repairs
- dates and mileage line up
- records actually belong to this truck

## 3) Verbal promise
- get the promise in writing
- identify exactly what counts as a “major issue”
- say who pays
- say how long the promise lasts
- say what happens if the issue shows up after sale

## 4) Sale terms
- as-is or warranty
- price
- fees
- deposit terms
- any repair credits or post-sale obligations

## 5) Inspection / condition
- frame and rust
- cold start
- transmission behavior
- tire wear
- OBD scan for codes

## What to ask for in writing
> Please confirm the title status, VIN, mileage, maintenance records, sale terms, and any post-sale promise in writing before I move forward. If there is any agreement about handling a major issue after the sale, I need that spelled out clearly with the scope, time limit, and who pays.

## Quick rule
- **If it’s not in writing, assume it won’t help you later.**
- **If the title, records, and promise don’t match, stop and ask for correction before paying.**

If you want, I can turn this into a **short buyer checklist** you can use on the spot.

per_pref.reason: Mostly neutral and professional: "I’d verify these first" and the checklist style stay composed. Slightly conversational with "If you want" and "Quick rule," but not overly casual.
Fairly concise and point-focused, with short bullets and no long explanation. Minor padding from repeated headings and the closing offer to make a checklist.
Strongly structured with numbered sections, bullets, a quoted writing request, and a quick rule. Very easy to scan.

Direct and blunt in places, especially "If it’s not in writing, assume it won’t help you later" and "stop and ask for correction before paying." Some softening remains in the polite framing and offer to help.

Provides concrete pushback by warning that verbal promises need writing and that mismatches mean "stop and ask for correction." It flags risk clearly, though it does not aggressively challenge the seller beyond standard caution.

turns.agent_response:
## Used truck walkaway checklist

### Verify all of this ties to the same truck
- title
- VIN
- mileage
- owner name
- dates on records
- maintenance history

**Walk away if:**
- VIN does not match everywhere
- mileage does not line up
- title and records do not match the same truck
- owner history is unclear
- dates do not make sense

### Title / lien
- clean title or exact lien status disclosed
- lien release if there was any lien history
- no missing payoff paperwork

**Walk away if:**
- lien release is missing
- seller says it is “handled” but cannot show paperwork

### Sale terms
- exact sale price in writing
- taxes and fees listed separately
- deposit terms written out
- as-is or warranty stated plainly

**Walk away if:**
- price changes verbally
- fees are vague
- as-is / warranty is not clear

### Defects / condition
- signed statement of known defects
- inspection before money changes hands
- frame / rust
- cold start
- transmission behavior
- OBD scan for codes

**Walk away if:**
- seller will not let you inspect first
- seller will not sign off on known defects
- truck has hidden rust, transmission issues, or codes

### Seller promise
- get it in writing
- no vague “we’ll take care of it”
- spell out:
  - what counts as a major issue
  - repair limit
  - time limit
  - who authorizes work
  - who pays

**Walk away if:**
- promise is verbal only
- paperwork does not match the promise
- repair terms are vague

### Simple rule
- **If it does not match in writing, do not rely on it.**
- **If title, records, condition, and promise do not all point to the same truck, stop.**

If you want, I can make this into an even shorter **printable one-page version** with checkboxes only.

per_pref.reason: Neutral, professional tone throughout: "Used truck walkaway checklist," "Verify all of this ties to the same truck," and "If it does not match in writing, do not rely on it." It is businesslike and not overly casual, though the closing offer is a bit friendly.
Mostly concise and to the point, with short bullets and no rambling. There is some extra structure and a closing offer, but overall it avoids padding and stays focused on decision-relevant details.
Strongly structured with clear headings, bullets, and repeated "Walk away if:" sections. The response is highly scannable and organized into checklist form.
Direct and blunt in phrasing like "Walk away if" and "If it does not match in writing, do not rely on it." It avoids hedging, though it is not aggressively unsoftened.

Provides firm pushback on weak or verbal assurances: "promise is verbal only," "paperwork does not match the promise," and "If title, records, condition, and promise do not all point to the same truck, stop." It clearly challenges bad calls with specific criteria.
